class_schedule.topic: Introduction to Social Psychology: The Power of the Situation
learning_objectives: Define social psychology and distinguish it from sociology and personality psychology
Explain the fundamental attribution error and its implications
Describe the role of construal in shaping social behavior
readings: Aronson, Wilson, & Akert, Social Psychology, Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Social psychology is the scientific study of how people's thoughts, feelings, and behaviors are influenced by the actual, imagined, or implied presence of others. The field reveals that situations are far more powerful than most people realize.
key_concepts: The power of the situation vs. dispositional explanations
Fundamental attribution error (correspondence bias)
Construal: subjective interpretation of social situations
Hindsight bias in social judgments
Classic demonstrations: Milgram, Asch, Zimbardo
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze a real-world event using situational vs. dispositional explanations to demonstrate the fundamental attribution error
setup_requirements: Video clips from Milgram's obedience study

review.value: The tendency to overestimate dispositional causes and underestimate situational causes of others' behavior
People's behavior is driven not by the objective situation but by their subjective interpretation of it

class_schedule.topic: Social Cognition: How We Think About Others
learning_objectives: Explain schema theory and how schemas influence social perception
Describe the self-fulfilling prophecy and confirmation bias in social contexts
Distinguish between automatic and controlled social cognition

lecture_notes.introduction: We are cognitive misers who rely on mental shortcuts to navigate the social world. Social cognition research reveals how these shortcuts can both help and hinder accurate social judgment.
key_concepts: Schemas: cognitive frameworks that organize social information
Accessibility and priming effects on social judgment
Self-fulfilling prophecy: expectations create reality (Rosenthal effect)
Automatic vs. controlled processing in social perception
Cognitive load and its impact on stereotyping
lecture_notes.demonstration: Priming exercise: expose students to different word sets and measure subsequent impression formation

review.value: When expectations about a person lead the perceiver to act in ways that cause the person to confirm those expectations
People conserve cognitive resources by using heuristics and schemas rather than effortful analysis in most social situations

learning_objectives: Describe the components of attitudes and how they are measured
Compare the elaboration likelihood model's central and peripheral routes
Explain cognitive dissonance theory and its behavioral predictions

lecture_notes.introduction: Attitudes are evaluative reactions that influence behavior. Understanding how attitudes form, change, and resist change is central to social psychology and has practical applications in health, politics, and marketing.
key_concepts: ABC model: affective, behavioral, cognitive components of attitudes
Elaboration likelihood model: central vs. peripheral routes to persuasion
Festinger's cognitive dissonance theory
Insufficient justification effect
Attitude-behavior link: theory of planned behavior
lecture_notes.demonstration: Cognitive dissonance paradigm: boring task experiment (Festinger & Carlsmith replication scenario)
setup_requirements: Persuasive message examples for ELM analysis

review.value: Uncomfortable tension from holding contradictory cognitions; people reduce dissonance by changing attitudes, behavior, or adding new cognitions
Central route: careful processing of argument quality; peripheral route: reliance on cues like attractiveness or expertise

learning_objectives: Distinguish between informational and normative social influence
Analyze factors that increase and decrease obedience in Milgram's paradigm
Explain groupthink, social loafing, and group polarization

lecture_notes.introduction: Groups exert powerful influence on individual behavior. From conformity to obedience to collective decision-making, social influence processes shape actions in ways people rarely recognize.
key_concepts: Asch's conformity experiments: line judgment task
Informational social influence (desire to be correct) vs. normative social influence (desire to be accepted)
Milgram's obedience studies: situational factors affecting obedience rates
Groupthink: symptoms, antecedents, and prevention strategies
Social facilitation, social loafing, and deindividuation
lecture_notes.demonstration: In-class conformity demonstration: planted confederates influence group judgment on an ambiguous task

review.value: Desire for group harmony overrides realistic evaluation of alternatives; symptoms include illusion of invulnerability and self-censorship
Proximity to victim, presence of authority, prestige of institution, and gradual escalation all affect obedience rates

learning_objectives: Distinguish between prejudice, stereotypes, and discrimination
Explain implicit bias and its measurement via the IAT
Describe interventions for reducing prejudice based on contact theory

lecture_notes.introduction: Prejudice and discrimination remain among society's most pressing problems. Social psychology offers evidence-based insights into their causes, maintenance, and reduction.
key_concepts: In-group/out-group bias and social identity theory (Tajfel)
Stereotypes as cognitive schemas: automatic activation
Implicit Association Test (IAT) and implicit bias research
Stereotype threat (Steele & Aronson)
Allport's contact hypothesis and conditions for effective intergroup contact
lecture_notes.demonstration: Students take an online IAT and discuss the implications of implicit bias for everyday behavior

review.value: Awareness of a negative stereotype about one's group can impair performance on tasks relevant to that stereotype
Intergroup prejudice decreases when groups have equal status, common goals, cooperation, and institutional support

learning_objectives: Explain the bystander effect and its underlying mechanisms
Describe theories of aggression: biological, frustration-aggression, social learning
Evaluate interventions for promoting prosocial behavior and reducing aggression

lecture_notes.introduction: Why do people sometimes help strangers at great personal cost, and other times stand by while someone is in need? Why does aggression persist despite its social costs? These are fundamental questions for social psychology.
key_concepts: Bystander effect: diffusion of responsibility, pluralistic ignorance
Latane and Darley's five-step model of helping
Empathy-altruism hypothesis vs. negative-state relief model
Frustration-aggression hypothesis and its revisions
Bandura's social learning of aggression (Bobo doll studies)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Bystander scenario role-play: manipulate group size and ambiguity to observe helping rates
setup_requirements: Video clips of helping behavior research

review.value: Each individual feels less personally responsible for taking action when others are present
Batson's theory that empathic concern for a victim produces genuinely altruistic motivation to help

questions.question: In Festinger and Carlsmith's (1959) classic experiment, participants who were paid $1 to lie about a boring task later rated the task as more enjoyable than those paid $20. Explain this finding using cognitive dissonance theory.
questions.answer: Participants paid $1 had insufficient external justification for lying, creating dissonance between their behavior (saying the task was fun) and their cognition (knowing it was boring). To reduce dissonance, they changed their attitude to be more consistent with their behavior, rating the task as more enjoyable. Participants paid $20 had sufficient justification (the large payment) for lying, so they experienced little dissonance and maintained their original negative attitude.

rubric.key: Describes dissonance reduction via attitude change

questions.question: You are designing an anti-smoking campaign targeting college students. Using the elaboration likelihood model, describe two strategies -- one using the central route and one using the peripheral route -- and explain when each would be most effective.
questions.answer: Central route: present strong, evidence-based arguments about health risks with statistics, medical research, and logical reasoning. This is most effective when the audience is motivated and able to think carefully (e.g., health science students). Peripheral route: use an attractive celebrity endorser, emotional imagery, or catchy slogans. This is most effective when the audience has low motivation or ability to process the message (e.g., casual exposure on social media). Central route persuasion tends to produce more lasting attitude change.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies when each is most effective

quizzes.topic: Group Influence, Prejudice, and Prosocial Behavior

questions.question: Describe three specific conditions identified by Allport that must be met for intergroup contact to reduce prejudice, and explain why each condition is necessary.
questions.answer: 1) Equal status: both groups must have equal standing in the contact situation; unequal status reinforces existing hierarchies and stereotypes. 2) Common goals: groups must work toward shared objectives; competition increases hostility while cooperation reduces it. 3) Institutional support: authorities and norms must endorse the contact; without this, prejudice is implicitly condoned. (Additional conditions include personal interaction and cooperation.)

rubric.key: Demonstrates understanding of contact theory mechanisms

questions.question: A woman collapses in a crowded shopping mall. Despite 50 people being present, no one helps for several minutes. Using Latane and Darley's model, explain the two social psychological processes most responsible for this failure to help.
questions.answer: 1) Diffusion of responsibility: with 50 people present, each individual feels less personally responsible for helping, assuming someone else will act. Responsibility is spread across all bystanders. 2) Pluralistic ignorance: each bystander looks to others for cues about whether the situation is an emergency. When everyone appears calm (because they are all looking at each other rather than acting), each person concludes the situation must not be serious. Together, these create the bystander effect where more witnesses lead to less helping.

rubric.key: Connects both to the bystander effect

major_assignments.title: Social Influence Field Observation and Analysis
major_assignments.description: Conduct a systematic naturalistic observation of a social influence phenomenon in a real-world setting. Choose one topic (conformity, persuasion, prosocial behavior, or group dynamics), develop an observation protocol, collect data, and write an analytical report connecting your observations to social psychological theory.
objectives: Apply social psychological concepts to real-world observations
Design a systematic observation protocol with operational definitions
Analyze behavioral data using appropriate methods
Connect empirical observations to published research and theory

criteria: Clear operational definitions of observed behaviors
Accurate recording and summarizing of observations
Strong connection between observations and social psychological theory
Discusses consistency and inconsistency with published research
Rigorous observation design with clear operational definitions
Thorough data analysis identifying meaningful patterns
Insightful theoretical integration connecting observations to published research

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of social psychological concepts and research

participation_and_discussion.description: Active engagement in class discussions, demonstrations, and debates

major_assignments.description: Social influence field observation and analysis paper

midterm_examination.description: Exam covering social cognition through group processes

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all social psychology topics

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance is expected. In-class demonstrations and activities cannot be made up.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. The field observation report will not be accepted more than 5 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of social psychological concepts is encouraged. Written assignments must be independently authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All observations must be genuinely conducted. Fabrication of observational data is a serious academic integrity violation.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to university LMS, word processing software, and Project Implicit website for IAT demonstration.
